MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant Nelson Williams Iii'S (1) Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication And (2) Joinder In City And County Of San Francisco'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Nov-04-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Betzy Bras-Gonzalez, Esq. (415.835.6777; bgongalez@bvlawsf.com) of Bracamontes & Vlasak, P.C. for Plaintiff, Patricia Ruiz.
Kevin Holl, Esq. (415.421.3100 x1003; Holl@gkhs.com) of Gordon-Creed, Kelley, Holl & Sugerman, LLP for Defendant, Nelson Williams, III.
Amy Super, ESq. (415.554.3849; amy.super@sfcityatty.org) Deputy City Attorney for Defendant, City and County of San Francisco.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT NELSON WILLIAMS' (1) MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication And (2) Joinder In City And County Of San Francisco'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication:

Defendant Williams' motion for summary judgment is denied. Mr. Williams' alternative motion for summary adjudication is granted in part and denied in part.

The motion is denied as to the first cause of action for racial harassment. Mr. Williams fails to maintain his burden of production. None of the nine facts in his separate statement of undisputed facts addresses this claim. The facts merely address the sexual harassment claim.

The motion is denied as to the second cause of action for sexual harassment based on the reasons and evidence set forth in CCSF's companion motion.

Worker's compensation does not bar these FEHA harassment claims because such conduct falls outside the employment compensation bargain. (See City of Moorpark v. Superior Ct. (1998) 18 Cal.4th 1143, 1155 [sexual and racial discrimination falls outside the compensation bargain]; Fretland v. County of Humboldt (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 1478, 1492.)

The motion is granted as to the sixth cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. As defendant correctly notes, most of Mr. Williams' sexually charged conduct was not directed toward plaintiff. The discrete conduct that Mr. Williams did direct towards plaintiff (e.g. "talking down" to her) is not outrageous as a matter of law. To be considered "outrageous," conduct must be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Davidson v. City of Westminster (1982) 32 Cal.3d 197, 209.) The conduct must be "directed at the plaintiff, or occur in the presence of a plaintiff of whom the defendant is aware." (Christensen v. Superior Court (1991) 54 Cal.3d 868, 903; see, e.g., Hughes v. Pair (2009) 46 Cal.4th 1035, 1050-1051 [affirming summary judgment on cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress despite "vulgar and highly offensive" sexual remark].)

The motion is granted as to the seventh cause of action for negligent infliction of emotional distress. Plaintiff's claims are predicated on intentional conduct. "An employer's supervisory conduct is inherently 'intentional.' In order to properly manage its business, every employer must on occasion review, criticize, demote, transfer and discipline employees." (Cole v. Fair Oaks Fire Protection District (1987) 43 Cal.3d 148, 160.) Where the conduct alleged is intentional, it cannot be used as a basis for a negligent infliction of emotional distress claim. (Semore v. Poole (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 1087, 1105.)

The motion is denied as to the claim for punitive damages. The alleged sexual harassment can support the claim.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-15-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Betzy Bras-Gonzalez, Esq. of BRACAMONTES & VLASAK, T:415.835.6777, for plaintiff;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANT NELSON WILLIAMS' MOTION TO STRIKE PORTIONS OF THE COMPLAINT as follows:

Defendant Nelson Williams III's motion to strike punitive damages from the complaint is denied. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is denied.

In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) "A court can award punitive damages in an FEHA case." (Myers v. Trendwest Resorts, Inc. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 1403, 1435-1437 [defendant employer failed to show entitlement to summary adjudication regarding punitive damages in sexual harassment case where supervisor was alleged to have created a hostile work environment].)

At the pleading stage, all Plaintiff must allege are specific facts showing that Defendant's conduct was oppressive, fraudulent or malicious. Plaintiff alleges Defendant Williams, her supervisor at the time, sexually and racially harassed her in the workplace. (Compl., 7.) The complaint alleges Defendant Williams created a hostile environment by calling Plaintiff "a "skank" and a "bitch", ogled and made sexual remarks about women, showed nude photographs of women, and bragged about travelling to the Caribbean to sleep with prostitutes." (Id.) Further, Plaintiff alleges Defendant Williams's conduct was knowing, intentional, and willful and that . Defendant Williams was fully aware that his conduct and comments would injure Plaintiff. (Compl., 65.) Thus, the complaint pleads punitive damages with the requisite factual specificity. Whether Plaintiff can prove the allegations by clear and convincing evidence is a matter for trial.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
